text: This is a video presentation of a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy. This was a seventy-year-old patient with a background history of laparotomy for bowel cancer who was found to have a twenty millimetre cyst in the body of the pancreas on a CT scan.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing adhesions and mobilizing the splenic flexure. The gastroesplenic ligament is then divided, along with the short gastric vessels, using surgical instruments.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, then dissects along the greater curvature of the stomach while preserving the gastroepiploic arcade, exposing the body of the pancreas.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection to expose the tail of the pancreas. The peritoneum is incised along the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing the confluence between the inferior mesenteric and splenic veins.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultrasound assessment, followed by the gentle dissection of the splenic artery, which is looped in a red sling for identification and control.
The surgeon lifts the body of the pancreas and creates a tunnel beneath it, securing control with a white sling. Subsequently, the splenic vein is isolated and looped with a blue sling.
The surgeon divides the splenic artery between haemlocks, followed by the division of the splenic vein using the same technique, ensuring secure vascular control.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to transect the body of the pancreas, ensuring clear margins. Subsequently, the pancreatic attachments to the retroperitoneum are divided while maintaining traction over the pancreas.

The surgeon divides the remaining splenic attachments from a medial to lateral direction, disconnecting the specimen. The specimen is placed in a large endo-catch bag, and a small five-centimeter incision is made over the previous laparotomy scar for specimen extraction.
